data.title: a16z: Intelligent agents begin to manipulate real systems. Who is the actual controller?
data.description:
Authors: Christian Catalini, Andy Hall, Noah Levine, Christian Crowley; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

AI agents have rapidly evolved from auxiliary assistants into major economic participants, developing at a pace far exceeding that of surrounding supporting infrastructure.

While AI agents can currently perform tasks and complete transactions, in cross-environment scenarios, they still lack standardized methods to prove their identity, operational permissions, and the path to obtain rewards. Identity cannot be transferred, payments lack programmability by default, and collaborative operations remain isolated.

Blockchain solves these problems at the infrastructure level. Public ledgers provide auditable credentials for every transaction, wallets grant agents transferable identities, and stablecoins offer an optional settlement layer. These are not foundational technologies of the future—they are ready for immediate use, enabling agents to operate autonomously and without permission as genuine economic participants. This article will explain how blockchain bridges these gaps in four dimensions: identity, payment, governance, and trust.

1. Non-human Agent Identity System

The bottleneck of the intelligent agent economy now lies in the identity system, not the level of intelligence..

In the financial services industry alone, the number of non-human agents—automated transaction systems, risk engines, and anti-fraud models—is already about 100 times that of human employees. And with the large-scale deployment of modern intelligent agent frameworks (large language models that can call tools, autonomous workflows, and multi-agent collaborative scheduling), this proportion will continue to rise across industries.

However, these intelligent agents are still essentially in a "bankless" state. They can access financial systems, but their interaction methods lack portability, verifiability, and default trust. They lack standardized mechanisms to prove their operational authority, operate independently across platforms, or take responsibility for their actions. What the industry currently lacks is a universal identity layer—equivalent to an SSL security protocol in the intelligent agent field—to standardize cross-platform collaboration processes. Although many well-known solutions have attempted to address this issue, the paths remain fragmented: on one side is a vertically integrated, fiat-centric technical architecture; on the other side are cryptographically native open standards (such as the x402 protocol and emerging intelligent agent identity proposals); and there are also developer framework extensions like the Model Context Protocol (MCP) that attempt to establish an application-layer identity system. To date, there is still no widely adopted, interoperable solution that allows one agent to prove to another who it represents, what it is allowed to do, and how it receives compensation. This is precisely the core concept of KYA (Know Your Agent). Just as humans rely on credit records and KYC (Know Your Customer) mechanisms, agents also need cryptographically signed credentials to bind them to their delegators, operational permissions, constraints, and reputation. Blockchain provides a neutral collaboration layer for this: transferable identities, programmable wallets, and verifiable claims that can be used across chat applications, APIs, and marketplaces. Early implementations have emerged in the industry: on-chain agent registries, agents based on native wallets (using USDC for settlement), the ERC standard for "least trusted agents," and developer toolkits that combine identity systems with embedded payments and anti-fraud controls. However, before a universal identity standard is introduced, merchants will still block intelligent agents from accessing the system at the firewall level. 2. Governance of AI-Driven Systems Intelligent agents are beginning to manipulate real systems, which raises a series of new questions: Who is the actual controller? Imagine a community or enterprise where an AI system coordinates core resources—whether it's financial allocation or supply chain management. Even if humans have the right to vote on policy changes, if the underlying AI layer is controlled by a single service provider, which can arbitrarily push model updates, adjust constraints, or overturn decisions, then human voice will become extremely weak. The governance layer in form may be decentralized, but the actual operational layer remains centralized—whoever controls the model ultimately controls the outcome. When intelligent agents assume governance functions, a new layer of dependency is introduced. Theoretically, this could make direct democracy more feasible: everyone could have an AI representative who interprets lengthy proposals, models and weighs pros and cons, and votes according to the explicit preferences of the principal. However, this vision hinges on these intelligent agents being truly accountable to the people they represent, being transferable across service providers, and technically constrained to obey human instructions. Otherwise, it will ultimately result in a system that appears democratic but is actually dominated by the behavior of a black-box model that is uncontrollable by humans. If the current reality is that intelligent agents are built from only a few basic models, we need a mechanism to prove that the agents are acting in the interests of the users, not the interests of the model companies. This will likely require providing cryptographic safeguards on multiple levels: Clearly defining the specific training data, fine-tuning methods, or reinforcement learning processes from which a model instance originates; Clearly defining the precise prompts and instructions governing a particular intelligent agent; Retaining all records of its behavior in the real world; Providing a reliable commitment that, after deployment, the service provider cannot arbitrarily modify the instructions or retrain the model without the user's knowledge. Without these safeguards, governance implemented by intelligent agents will ultimately degenerate into governance implemented by the party controlling the model's weights. This is precisely where cryptography can play a crucial role. If collective decisions are recorded on-chain and executed automatically, AI systems must adhere to verified results; if agents possess cryptographic identities and transparent execution logs, their representatives can be verified to ensure they are acting within authorized limits; if the AI ​​layer is user-owned and portable, rather than tied to a single platform, no single company can arbitrarily change the rules by updating the model. Ultimately, governing AI systems is fundamentally an infrastructure issue, not a policy issue. True power depends on building enforceable and trustworthy safeguards within the system.

3. Filling the Gaps in the Traditional Payment System for AI-Native Businesses

AI agents have begun to autonomously engage in consumption—for example, web scraping, browser sessions, and image generation—and stablecoins are gradually becoming an alternative settlement layer for these transactions. At the same time, a new type of transaction market oriented towards intelligent agents is taking shape. Take Stripe and Tempo's MPP marketplace as an example. This platform aggregates over 60 services designed specifically for AI agents, processing over 34,000 transactions in its first week with fees as low as $0.003. Stablecoins are also listed as one of the default payment methods. The core difference lies in how these services are accessed: none of them have a checkout page. The agent reads the data structure, sends a request, completes the payment, and receives the result—the entire process is completed in a single interaction. They represent a new type of "headless merchant"—consisting only of a server, a set of interface endpoints, and a single-call pricing mechanism, without any front-end interface, online store, or sales team. The payment infrastructure supporting all of this is now operational. Coinbase's x402 protocol uses a different technical path than MPP, but both embed payment functionality directly into HTTP requests. Visa is also expanding its card payment network in a similar direction through a command-line tool, allowing developers to initiate payments directly on the terminal, while merchants receive stablecoin settlements instantly in the backend. Related data is still in its early stages. After excluding fraudulent transactions such as fake transactions, x402 currently processes approximately $1.6 million in agent-driven payments per month, far lower than the $24 million reported by Bloomberg recently, citing data from x402.org. However, its surrounding infrastructure is expanding rapidly: Stripe, Cloudflare, Vercel, and Google have all integrated x402 into their platforms. Developer tools represent a significant opportunity, with vibe coding expanding the boundaries of the software development community and thus increasing the overall potential market size for development tools. Companies like Merit Systems are building the infrastructure for this trend with AgentCash, a command-line wallet and marketplace platform that integrates with both MPP and x402 protocols. These products allow agents to use stablecoins in a unified account balance to purchase the data, tools, and capabilities they need. For example, a sales team's AI agent can refine sales lead information by calling a single interface endpoint, combining data from Apollo, Google Maps, and Whitepages, all without the user leaving the command-line interface. Besides emerging bank card-based solutions, there are several reasons why these AI agents tend to use encrypted payment channels for commercial transactions. Firstly, risk control and credit granting. Payment service providers need to assume the corresponding business risks when integrating merchants. For headless merchants without independent websites or legal entities, traditional payment service providers find it difficult to complete risk assessment and credit granting. Secondly, stablecoins enable permissionless programmability in open networks: any developer can set up payment functionality for the interface endpoint without needing to connect to a payment processor or sign a merchant agreement. We have seen similar industry trends before. Every change in business transaction models gives rise to a new type of merchant that is initially difficult for existing systems to adapt to. Companies building this kind of infrastructure are not betting on the current monthly transaction volume of $1.6 million, but on the market size that can be achieved when intelligent agents become the mainstream transacting parties. 4. Reconstructing Trust Pricing in the Intelligent Agent Economy For 300,000 years, human cognition has been the core bottleneck restricting development. Now, AI is pushing the marginal cost of execution to zero. When a scarce resource becomes abundant, the constraint shifts. When intelligence becomes cheap, what becomes expensive? Validation. In the intelligent agent economy, the real limitation to scalability lies in our biologically limited ability to audit and assess the risks of machine decisions. The processing power of intelligent agents has long surpassed the regulatory limits of humans. Due to the high cost of regulation and the lag in risk outbreaks, the market naturally tends to underinvest in this area. "Humans in the loop" is rapidly becoming physically infeasible. However, deploying unverified intelligent agents brings cumulative risks. Systems can coldly optimize around "alternative metrics," silently deviating from human intent, creating a false appearance of productivity, and masking the accumulation of massive AI debt. To safely entrust the economy to machines, trust can no longer rely on human verification—trust must be hard-coded into the system architecture itself. When anyone can generate content for free, the most important thing is verifiable origin—knowing where it comes from and whether it is trustworthy. Blockchain, along with on-chain credentials and decentralized digital identity systems, expands the economic boundaries for secure AI deployment. We no longer need to treat AI as a black box, but can obtain a clear and auditable history of its behavior. As more AI agents begin to transact with each other, settlement networks and traceability of origin become increasingly intertwined. Systems that handle funds—such as stablecoins and smart contracts—can also carry cryptographic credentials, recording who did what and who is responsible when problems arise. Human comparative advantage will shift upwards: from correcting minor errors to setting strategic directions and taking responsibility when systems fail. Sustainable competitive advantage belongs to those entities that can provide cryptographic proof of their outputs, insure them, and take responsibility for failures. Unverified scaling is a liability that accumulates over time. 5. Ensuring User Control

5. Ensuring User Control

For decades, layers of abstraction have defined how users interact with technology. Programming languages ​​abstracted machine code, command lines were replaced by graphical user interfaces, and then evolved into mobile applications and APIs. Each change has hidden more underlying complexity while always keeping users firmly in control.

In an agent-driven world, users only specify the target result, not the specific steps, and the system autonomously determines the implementation path. Intelligent agents are not merely abstract methods of task execution, but also abstract the subjects that perform those tasks. After setting initial parameters, users can relinquish control, and the system will operate autonomously. The user's role shifts from direct interaction to supervision and management; without user intervention, the system defaults to continuous operation. As users delegate more and more tasks to intelligent agents, new risks emerge: **Ambiguous input may cause the agent to act based on incorrect assumptions without the user's awareness; malfunctions may be concealed, making it impossible to clearly troubleshoot problems; a simple authorization may trigger unexpected, multi-step, complex processes.** This is precisely where encryption technology comes into play. The core of encryption technology is always to minimize blind trust. When users delegate more decision-making power to software, intelligent agent systems exacerbate this problem and place higher demands on design rigor—through setting clearer permission boundaries, increasing transparency, and providing stronger constraints on system behavior. A new generation of native encryption tools has emerged to address this need. Delegation frameworks with defined scopes, such as the MetaMask delegation toolkit, Coinbase AgentKit and smart agent wallets, and Merit Systems' AgentCash, allow users to define the boundaries of smart agent permissions at the smart contract level. Intent-based architectures, such as NEAR Intents (which has processed over $15 billion in decentralized exchange trading volume since Q4 2024), allow users to simply set a goal, such as "cross-chain tokens and staking," without specifying concrete execution steps. A new internet infrastructure where smart agents directly participate in economic activities is under construction. The unresolved question is: will this system be designed with extreme transparency, accountability, and user control as its guiding principles, or will it be built on a system not originally designed for non-human entities?

data.title: Token Computing: The Cost War of the Next Decade
data.description:
The computing power currency Token is reshaping the value coordinates of the AI ​​era. This article traces a complete path of change: from changes in charging methods to the differentiation of cost structures, and then to the adjustment of budget systems, to see how the Token economy is being redefined.

Author: Li Biao

“Token economy” is becoming one of the hottest keywords in the AI ​​industry recently.

data.title: Galaxy: API Payment in the Era of Contactless SaaS and Artificial Intelligence
data.description:
Author: Vikram Singh, Source: Galaxy, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Overview

  • Today's software payment system is built for static, manually driven partnerships. For new developers who have never written a single line of code, configuring and managing APIs/MCPs is extremely cumbersome. High-quality data sources are constantly losing customers because large language models cannot dynamically interact with paywalls.

The Development History of SaaS to Date

The past few weeks have seen noteworthy industry developments from the perspective of smart agent payments: Tempo launched its mainnet, with the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) performing particularly well; Ramp launched smart agent cards last week; Visa released command-line tools and is actively promoting its smart payment business; Coinbase has also been leading the development of the x402 protocol in recent months.

However, the industry focus is currently mostly on commercial shopping scenarios within Large Language Model (LLM) chat interfaces, with very limited discussion on software and data payments. I believe that AI will fundamentally change our future understanding of software payments and the SaaS model, and this article will explore this viewpoint.

The first software licensing agreement was signed in 1969 when IBM decided to split its large and expensive hardware and software services for sale.

Years later, the software finance sector is now dominated by the SaaS model, which can be further divided into high-involvement (e.g., Salesforce, SAP) and low-involvement (e.g., X, Stripe). High-involvement SaaS has a high average order value, high customer acquisition cost per new user, and high customer support costs; low-involvement SaaS has a lower average order value and lower customer acquisition cost per new user, but overall customer acquisition investment remains high to acquire a large number of users. With the unstoppable development of AI, the software payment system is reaching a turning point, potentially giving rise to the contactless SaaS model I define. The gradually disappearing programming barriers and the birth of contactless SaaS: Programming tools have lowered the barrier to entry and will continue to do so, covering a wider range of "developers" with limited programming experience. We can fully foresee that within just a few years, programming experience will no longer be a barrier to software development—in fact, this trend is already evident. Currently, the minor obstacles to developing personal or non-enterprise software (aside from a small amount of operational knowledge) mainly focus on: finding suitable data sources, tools, and APIs; configuring API keys and completing service payments; and integrating them into one's own application. The third point has been abstracted and simplified by tools such as Claude Code and Codex, while the first two points still have significant room for innovation and optimization (to some extent, machine payment protocols have already brought about related changes). The current process of configuring API keys is cumbersome and inefficient: ① Find the corresponding API → ② Create an account → ③ Complete identity verification via email verification code → ④ Add a payment method → ​​⑤ Generate an API key → ⑥ Store the key → ⑦ Configure call rate limits → ⑧ Set up billing reminders → ⑨ Write code. This process cannot be skipped, and it needs to be repeated for each new API, tool, or data source. The process of establishing a machine payment protocol connection is similar. For high-value integration scenarios like Stripe and X, the cognitive cost of API selection and the resistance of the configuration process can be amortized through hundreds of thousands of API calls, so this cumbersome process is acceptable; however, for low-frequency usage scenarios, this process is too redundant.

Source: Galaxy Ventures

Leaving aside the mindset of skeuomorphic design, let's think about what the discovery and retrieval of contextual data sources should look like. Suppose I ask my large language model, "What happened in New York today?" It should be able to find the New York Times API, access the relevant API endpoints, and retrieve the relevant news articles for me. Such a one-off API call should not require me to create a Machine Payment Protocol (MCP) connection for a trivial request. Similarly, if I were to posed a broader query: “Write an investment analysis report for stock {ABC}. Consult SEC filings (using the Financial Datasets API), latest news (using the Parallel Web Systems API), and social sentiment (Twitter/X API).” This would involve 3 MCP connections, thus requiring 3 bilateral billing relationships to be established for a single query. This user experience (UX) is terrible and can be improved through aggregation and centralization. Beyond contextual data sources and our high-level large language model workflow, let’s consider how the experience for non-enterprise developers will evolve. Suppose you’ve developed a web application that provides the above stock analysis workflow and want to roll it out to a user base of 2,500 customers—not enterprise-scale, but certainly not small. To ensure your application runs stably and without crashing in a production environment, you might need to upgrade your services like Financial Datasets, Parallel, and Twitter/X to higher-priced plans, causing your server costs (Vercel, Supabase, Railway, etc.) to skyrocket. This means you've accumulated (and locked in) significant costs to provide the service. At this point, you have the following options: Pay the high upfront costs yourself, but the revenue stream is uncertain; Seek venture capital, but your semi-formal, amateur project is unlikely to attract funding; Have your customers pay based on real-time usage. The cost calculation method for real-time payment can be: User bundling cost = Cumulative API cost (Financial Datasets, Parallel, Twitter/X) + Cumulative server cost (Vercel, Supabase, Railway) + Value-added/Platform service fee. Real-time payment can theoretically solve the cold start problem that requires prepayment and supports dynamic pricing models (adjusting fees based on supply and demand, and optimizing pricing packages and server space based on real-time usage). Content, Data, and Aggregation Let's look at two cases: Case 1 "In-depth research into the US GPU and data center market. Who are the most important players? What are the chip manufacturers' import and export volumes? Where are new data centers being built?" This type of search depth is structurally limited, and the results are often mediocre. It can only access the free version of SemiAnalysis and other free data sources, while truly high-quality data is hidden behind paywalls. Case Study 2: Using the same prompt as Case Study 1 + "This research is important to me. You have a budget of $10, please use the best data source." Source: Galaxy Ventures Assuming the data source supports pay-as-you-go API endpoints, the large language model can autonomously retrieve these data sources, complete payments using its budget, and leverage high-value information to refine in-depth research. To achieve the same effect as Case 2 in Case 1, human users must manually find data sources, configure keys, and establish billing relationships with various API/MCP service providers. In Case 2, the large language model dynamically retrieves and pays for the optimal data source, saving humans the costs of searching, decision-making, and repetitive configuration. As a side note, but crucially: the above examples reveal a fundamental misalignment in the data and contextual data source discovery process. The exploration costs of finding suitable APIs and the decision-making costs of determining whether an API is worth paying for are shifted from the end user to the large language model's retrieval engine and budget constraints. From this perspective, the large language model is becoming the core aggregation layer for content and data. Why hasn't AI budgeting become widespread yet? Implementing AI budgets requires calling APIs while the large language model is running, and runtime API calls demand atomicity in payments. For example, if you pay $5 to call the XYZ API for a task, how can you guarantee that XYZ will actually provide the service and not simply take the money? If payments and data acquisition can be done atomically, we can have a trustless system. To scale this, we need micropayments. However, the existing financial infrastructure is structurally incapable of supporting micropayments. Credit cards charge approximately 2.9% + $0.30 in fees per transaction. Assuming a call cost of $0.01, the fixed fees alone are 30 times the API cost. Based on these rates, the minimum feasible transaction amount with fees less than 10% of the cost is $4.29. However, credit cards can serve as an abstraction layer for micropayments (explained in detail later). Therefore, pay-per-API pricing is inherently unsuitable for existing payment channels. The industry can only solve API payment issues through bulk billing: pre-deposited funds, monthly bills, and subscription packages. You won't see 100,000 individual $0.001 transactions in a Claude Code call; Anthropic will only deduct $100 from your pre-deposited account. Similarly, Amazon doesn't bill each AWS Lambda call individually, but instead issues a single monthly bill. This model works, so why change it? First, in both scenarios, you handle the retrieval process yourself, cost planning is your (or your company's) decision-making burden, and the architecture design of the call method is also determined by you. Each new API requires establishing a new static bilateral partnership. Each billing cycle involves pre-paying for the services expected to be used. In the world of dynamic retrieval, establishing static partnerships is a major bottleneck. Prepaying funds is meaningless when you don't even know which API you'll use. This necessitates a new infrastructure: one that can establish partnerships without breaking trust, and one that can facilitate payments free from the constraints of outdated pricing systems. A natural solution is blockchain-based micropayments. But have micropayments never succeeded historically? Digicash, Flooz, Beenz, and Blendle are all failures in the micropayment field. However, as Nick Szabo argues in his article "The Psychological Barriers to Micropayments," micropayments were destined to fail from the start—the cost of human decision-making and record-keeping is usually higher than the actual transaction cost. Simply put, humans value predictability more than accuracy. A $15 monthly Spotify subscription, even if only half-used, is psychologically superior to a pay-as-you-go model that might ultimately cost only $7.50. The former has lower psychological transaction costs, while the latter requires multiple thought processes. This is why subscriptions win: they transform a series of small decisions with a fixed average value into a single, predictable large decision (which allows service providers to charge more than the actual usage value). This is also why "waste" is common in SaaS pricing—users often pay more than they actually use. However, this logic no longer holds true when economic agents shift from humans to large language models/AI agents. AI agents do not experience decision fatigue; their "psychological accounting costs" are not measured by cognitive effort but by reasoning computational power. The only constraint on the agent is the budget allocated by humans, which can be simplified to a simple cost minimization function. Achieving Contactless SaaS: Low-intervention SaaS reduces customer acquisition costs by eliminating sales teams. Contactless SaaS, on the other hand, allows tools and data sources to be directly retrieved and atomically paid, completely eliminating the customer acquisition process. This requires three infrastructure changes: Machine-readable price discovery: This is equivalent to forming an API marketplace. It can be understood as adding a layer of request headers or metadata containing standardized pricing information on top of an MCP that already has the ability to describe functions. Skills.sh leaderboards and plugin marketplaces have already shown this prototype.

  • Payment and API Abstraction: Currently, "one-click retrieval of paid data" remains largely theoretical, as most services still require the creation of API keys. MCP achieves abstraction to some extent, with Coinbase's x402 protocol and Tempo's Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) being particularly valuable in this area.

  • Instant Settlement: Due to structural requirements, settlement must be fast and low-cost. It is well known that the speed of fund transfers is much slower than that of software. This is usually not a bottleneck, but in this scenario, ultra-fast payments are crucial. If the payment completion speed is slower than the data return speed, the service provider bears the credit risk brought by the intelligent agent, thus requiring intermediary intervention. Atomicity requirements set a lower limit for data retrieval latency, and this lower limit is determined by the execution speed of the underlying payment channel.

    • Discussion on the Economic Model of Contactless SaaS

    As Patrick McKenzie (@patio11) of Stripe stated, the most basic revenue formula for SaaS is:

    Revenue = Σ(Customer Acquisition × Conversion Rate × Average Revenue per User) / Churn Rate

    High-involvement SaaS maximizes average revenue per user through six-figure transaction amounts per customer. Low-involvement SaaS maximizes customer acquisition and conversion efficiency through product-driven growth, freemium models, and low barriers to entry.

    In a contactless model, customer acquisition costs theoretically approach zero—unless there are methods like bribing agents or paying to improve API rankings—because agents automatically search for resources.

    The conversion rate is also almost 100% because retrieval and payment are atomically completed simultaneously. However, the average revenue per user drops significantly: revenue is no longer the $15 monthly subscription fee, but a single call fee of $0.0X, with extremely volatile call volume. In this model, the unit economics model only holds true when transaction volume scales up. A service with a single call fee of $0.003 requires 5,000 calls per month to break even with the $15 subscription fee. There are still many issues in this area worth exploring, which I will discuss in subsequent articles. Finally, some macro-level thoughts and open questions: With instant onboarding and higher searchability, service providers can serve millions of intelligent agents simultaneously, rather than thousands of human subscribers. Therefore, even if revenue per user decreases, the number of "users" may grow exponentially. Contactless SaaS requires participants to accept a low-margin, high-transaction-volume model. Currently, is the appeal of dynamic retrieval using large language models sufficient to persuade enterprises to shift from subscription-based to pay-as-you-go pricing? To achieve large-scale real-time pay-as-you-go pricing, the cost of the underlying payment channel must be extremely low to avoid eroding already meager profits. How should value be distributed across different layers? Can the large language model aggregation layer (Anthropic/OpenAI) include APIs in its catalog and charge platform fees? What is the profit margin for the financial layer/blockchain (structurally unable to charge high fees)? How should the authorization and real-name authentication process be designed? How to distinguish between genuine large language model API calls and Sybil attacks? What role do credit cards play in this (MPP supports credit cards as a payment method)? How does session-based payment actually work? Who bears the credit risk? Virtual cards are a direction worth paying attention to. A New Payment Paradigm Contactless SaaS reveals a completely new software payment paradigm: the retrieval layer shifts from humans to intelligent agents, and the decision-making cost of service selection shifts from human emotion to a quantified cost-benefit trade-off. Since data acquisition and payment need to be atomically synchronized, real-time payment becomes a necessity. In the next article, I will delve into how these trends reshape API bundling models, how profits are distributed at each level, and what form a fully native intelligent agent economy will actually take in reality.

data.title: Iran plans to enact a law governing the Strait of Hormuz; Vercel discloses security incident.
data.description:
Headline

▌Iran Plans to Introduce Strait of Hormuz Management Law, Banning Israeli-Affiliated Vessels

On April 20th, local time on the 19th, Rezaei Kochi, Chairman of the Civil Engineering Committee of the Iranian Parliament, stated that Iran has drafted a comprehensive law on the management of the Strait of Hormuz, which is nearing finalization. According to this law, the passage of goods and vessels affiliated with the Israeli regime will be prohibited. Belligerent nations will also be prohibited from passing through without permission from Iran's Supreme National Security Council. Furthermore, any country that has caused damage to Iran will be prohibited from passing through the Strait of Hormuz until compensation is paid. Under the plan, vessels from all countries must pay a fee in rials to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. 30% of the toll revenue will be used to strengthen the armed forces, and 70% will be used to improve the lives of the people.

(CCTV) Vercel disclosed a "limited" security incident on April 19th, stating that it had discovered an unauthorized access to some of Vercel's internal systems. An investigation is underway, and incident response experts have been hired to assist with the investigation and remediation. Vercel has notified law enforcement and will provide updates on the investigation's progress. Vercel has identified a limited number of affected customers directly involved; official services remain operational and will continue to be updated with the latest information. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $73,870.07, a 24-hour change of -2.5%; ETH price is $2,267.67, a 24-hour change of -3.7%; BNB price is $617.40, a 24-hour change of -2.1%. SOL price is $83.55, a 24-hour change of -3.2%; DOGE price is $0.09301, a 24-hour change of -2.0%; XRP price is $1.40, a 24-hour change of -2.8%; TRX price is $0.3295, a 24-hour change of +0.1%. The price of WLFI is $0.07734, a 24-hour change of -2.3%; the price of HYPE is $40.88, a 24-hour change of -7.1%.

Policy

▌Russia Plans to Strictly Regulate Crypto Trading: Unlicensed Operations Could Result in Up to 7 Years of Forced Labor

On April 19, a draft bill published by the Russian State Duma revealed that Russia plans to introduce criminal liability for unlicensed cryptocurrency trading and exchange operations. Organizing digital currency trading activities without the central bank's permission will be considered a crime, and related operators could face up to 7 years of forced labor. Crypto miners who fail to declare industrial mining activities will also be punished. The draft bill also stipulates that individuals who buy or sell crypto assets without a license may also face criminal penalties, including fines (approximately $1,300 to $4,000) or up to 4 years of forced labor; while operators of large exchanges could face fines of up to $13,000 and possible imprisonment for 5 to 7 years. It is understood that if the bill is passed, it could take effect as early as July 1, 2027.

...

▌Iranian Media: Iran Refuses to Participate in Second Round of Iran-US Talks

Golden Finance reported on April 20 that the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA) released an English-language message on social media on April 19, stating that Iran refused to participate in the second round of talks with the United States. (Xinhua News Agency)

▌Iran Designates New Shipping Route in Strait of Hormuz; Passage Prohibited Without Permission

On April 20, according to CCTV, local time on April 19, Iranian sources reported that the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy had designated a new shipping route from south of Hormuz Island to south of Larak Island. It is understood that this route is named the "Larak Corridor," and passage is prohibited without the permission of the Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy.

... Blockchain Applications ▌EasyDNS Admits Responsibility for eth.limo Domain Hijacking Incident, First Social Engineering Attack in 28 Years On April 20th, ENS web gateway eth.limo released a post-incident analysis report on Saturday, indicating that the DNS hijacking incident that occurred Friday night stemmed from a social engineering attack targeting its domain registrar, EasyDNS. The DNSSEC verification mechanism rejected the attacker's modifications to the name server, and eth.limo stated that no impact on users has been found so far. EasyDNS publicly acknowledged the security incident, stating that this is the first successful social engineering attack against a customer in the registrar's 28-year history. Given the increasing number of cryptocurrency front-end projects being compromised at the DNS layer in recent months, eth.limo will migrate to a more stringent EasyDNS-affiliated service provider that does not support account recovery.

▌Polygon: Polygon Chain, Agglayer, and the Entire Ecosystem Unaffected by the rsETH Vulnerability Event

On April 20th, Polygon posted on the X platform, stating, "The team has been actively monitoring the rsETH vulnerability event: the Polygon chain, Agglayer, and the broader ecosystem, including Katana and Vaultbridge, have not been affected by the event. To date, Polygon has safely transferred over $2 trillion, and we will continue to remain vigilant and monitor the development of the event."

Cryptocurrency

▌$138 Million Liquidated Across the Network in the Past 4 Hours, Primarily Long Positions Liquidated

▌Due to KelpDAO attackers depositing rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, a large number of whales have begun withdrawing ETH from Aave

On April 19th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, due to the KelpDAO vulnerability incident, attackers deposited rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, resulting in bad debts on Aave. ▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, the price of gold rises, while the price of Bitcoin plummets. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from obtaining a safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeated behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-prone asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.
